definition: Over 90% of institutional loans are covenant-lite with EBITDA addbacks averaging 25-40%, eliminating maintenance covenant early-warning systems. This creates bimodal default distribution: either successful refinancing or liquidity crisis with limited recovery, bypassing gradual deterioration. True leverage is 5.5-7.5x (not reported 4.0-4.5x) and true coverage is 2.5-3.5x (not reported 4.0-4.5x).
evidence: Identified in pair_1 debate as most important insight either agent produces
S&P LCD: covenant-lite first-lien loans defaulted at 2.2% cumulative vs 3.8% covenant-heavy through COVID
COVID tested covenant-lite under unprecedented policy support, not organic deterioration
Mechanism for suppression: eliminating false-positive technical defaults
Mechanism for bimodality: no early warning triggers until distress is severe
n=0 full cycles without policy rescue; interaction with higher funding costs untested
open_questions: How will bimodal default distribution affect recovery rates in the next downturn?
Are rating agencies adjusting for EBITDA addback inflation?
How will covenant-lite perform under organic deterioration without policy rescue?
Does interaction with higher absolute funding costs produce different outcomes?
Are recovery rates meaningfully lower for covenant-lite defaults?
Does covenant-lite reduce aggregate defaults or just delay and concentrate them?
